title: Women In Garbage

jokes.text: How old is that handkerchief? Well, it's not a handkerchief. It's a receipt.
jokes.setup: Leslie mistakes Paula's old receipt for an ancient handkerchief
jokes.punchline: Paula matter-of-factly reveals it's just a receipt, implying she's held onto it forever
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with good economy. Reveals character detail about Paula's age/habits through misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is beautiful. What is this fabric? Is it linen?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: That's a Target receipt from 1987.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You've been carrying this around for... how long?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: I don't know. A while.

jokes.text: I'm not allowed to reserve this conference room without my husband or father's signature.
jokes.setup: Leslie is discussing how far women have come in Pawnee
jokes.punchline: Reveals an absurd bureaucratic rule that shows how little has actually changed
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic contrast that's both funny and makes a point. Very Leslie to remain optimistic while citing ridiculous inequality.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Women have come so far in Pawnee. We've broken through glass ceilings, shattered barriers, and now we hold positions of real power and influence.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But there's still one thing we can't do: approve a pothole repair without getting signed off on by three male department heads, a notary public, and the ghost of the town's founder.

jokes.text: wore mirrors on their shoes to look up my skirt
jokes.setup: Paula listing inappropriate behavior from male colleagues
jokes.punchline: Escalates from cigar smoking to increasingly specific sexual harassment
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that works through specific absurd detail. Escalation builds comedy while addressing serious topic.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: So first there was the cigar smoking in the office, which is fine, whatever. Then he started making comments about my appearance. Then he cornered me in the supply closet and tried to kiss me. Then he showed me his—

jokes.text: except for Councilman Dexhart, who's a self-described pervert
jokes.setup: Leslie declaring the government is now 'pervert-free'
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts herself by mentioning the one guy who openly admits to being a pervert
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie optimism paired with immediate contradiction. 'Self-described pervert' is brilliantly specific and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The government is now pervert-free!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Except for Jerry. He's still here.

jokes.text: All the other councilmen used to keep a calendar of my menstrual cycle.
jokes.setup: Discussion of workplace harassment
jokes.punchline: Shockingly specific and invasive behavior presented matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Disturbingly funny through its specificity and Paula's casual delivery. Strong cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Admittedly, they were right. Because of the calendar.
jokes.setup: Paula mentions being dismissed for hormonal reasons during her period
jokes.punchline: Deadpan admission that they were technically correct due to their creepy tracking
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that makes the previous disturbing joke even funnier through Paula's matter-of-fact acceptance.

dialogue.speaker: Paula Peverley
dialogue.line: They said I was being dismissed for hormonal reasons during my period.

dialogue.speaker: Paula Peverley
dialogue.line: But they were technically correct. I do keep a calendar.

jokes.text: I think men are better than women.
jokes.setup: Serious discussion about gender equality
jokes.punchline: April's deadpan contrarian statement
jokes.explanation: Perfect April character moment - saying the most provocative thing possible in deadpan style.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I don't believe in gender equality. I think women are already superior and I'm tired of people pretending it's still a debate.

jokes.text: Leslie, you'll never land a beau with that domineering tone.
jokes.setup: April continuing her vintage sexist persona
jokes.punchline: Using old-fashioned word 'beau' while critiquing Leslie's tone
jokes.explanation: Word choice 'beau' and 'domineering tone' perfectly capture the vintage sexist voice April is adopting.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Your beau needs to work on his tone. He sounds like he's constantly angry at a sandwich.

jokes.text: We must always walk behind the men. Sheugh.
jokes.setup: April's continued vintage sexism performance
jokes.punchline: Caps it off with disgusted grunt at her own words
jokes.explanation: The 'Sheugh' perfectly breaks character and shows April's disgust at her own performance.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Women shouldn't be allowed to vote or own property. We're too emotional and our brains are smaller.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: *disgusted grunt*

jokes.text: Moustache! Dumb-dumb!
jokes.setup: Kids playing and being kids
jokes.punchline: Innocent kid insults delivered with enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Simple kid humor that works through innocent delivery of mild insults.

dialogue.line: Innocent kid insults delivered with enthusiasm

jokes.text: I borrowed some markers from Leslie and some stickers from Leslie and a Lite-Brite which I got from Andy.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining how he's entertaining the kids
jokes.punchline: Reveals he had to borrow children's toys from his coworkers
jokes.explanation: Funny that macho Ron had to borrow kids' supplies, and that Andy apparently has a Lite-Brite at work.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm entertaining the kids by letting them play with toys I borrowed from you people at work.

jokes.text: Hey, dudettes. You stoked about the weekend?
jokes.setup: Ann trying to connect with kids
jokes.punchline: Uses embarrassingly outdated slang
jokes.explanation: Ann's awkwardness with kids shown through cringey slang. Very in-character.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Hey kids, what's up? That's tight! That's da bomb! We can kick it, fo shizzle!

jokes.text: Oh, look at this pwetty, pwetty wittle beads.
jokes.setup: Ann continuing to struggle with kid interaction
jokes.punchline: Baby talks to kids who are clearly too old for it
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor from Ann's misguided attempt at kid-friendly communication.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Hey there, little guy! Ooh, who's a precious baby? You are! Yes, you are!

dialogue.speaker: Kid
dialogue.line: I'm nine.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Oh... well, who's a precious nine-year-old? You are!

jokes.text: They're not infants.
jokes.setup: Ann's baby talk to older kids
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact correction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron response - direct, practical, shutting down Ann's misguided approach.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Ann, you know that babies can't understand words, right? They just respond to tone and inflection. So talking to them like they're adults is actually more effective.

jokes.text: So you guys like Coldplay?
jokes.setup: Ann desperately trying to find common ground with kids
jokes.punchline: Asks young kids about adult alternative rock band
jokes.explanation: Shows Ann's complete disconnect from what kids would be interested in.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: So, uh, do you guys like The Strokes?

jokes.text: You're weird.
jokes.setup: Ann's awkward attempts at connecting
jokes.punchline: Kid's blunt assessment
jokes.explanation: Kids' brutal honesty provides perfect punchline to Ann's awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Kid
dialogue.line: You're weird.

jokes.text: Recently, we engaged in something called a 'group hang.'
jokes.setup: Chris trying to understand modern dating
jokes.punchline: Treats 'group hang' like an anthropological discovery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris character moment - analyzing social situations like scientific phenomena.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Wait, so a 'group hang' is when multiple people just... hang out together? Without any formal structure or predetermined outcome?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: That's fascinating. It's like observing a previously undocumented social ritual. Do they have a leader? A hierarchy? What are the resource allocation protocols?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: This changes everything I thought I knew about modern courtship.

jokes.text: 'Warning: Toxic bleach' is a good label.
jokes.setup: Chris philosophizing about relationship labels
jokes.punchline: Compares relationship status to hazardous material warnings
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd analogy that's both funny and reveals Chris's logical but off-kilter thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know, I think relationship labels are like hazardous material warnings. They're just there to protect people from something dangerous.

jokes.text: Shauna Malwae-Tweep has amazing dimples.
jokes.setup: Serious discussion about the commission
jokes.punchline: Chris randomly compliments Shauna's physical features
jokes.explanation: Classic Chris non-sequitur that shows his tendency to get distracted by positive observations.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know, Shauna, I have to say... those are some really nice shoulders you have. Very well-defined. And your bone structure is just... exceptional.

jokes.text: I know this text you sent me was a lie and Michael Stipe is not actually here, correct?
jokes.setup: Andy arriving at Parks department
jokes.punchline: Reveals someone lied about R.E.M. lead singer being there
jokes.explanation: Good setup showing Andy's gullibility and someone's willingness to exploit it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, where's Michael Stipe? I thought he was supposed to be here today!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, about that... nobody actually said Michael Stipe was coming.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? But I've been preparing all morning! I learned all the lyrics to 'It's the End of the World as We Know It'!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Andy, we lied to you so you'd actually show up on time for work.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You guys are the worst.

jokes.text: I just couldn't live with myself if it had been true.
jokes.setup: Andy knowing the Michael Stipe text was a lie
jokes.punchline: But admitting he had to check anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - knows it's fake but can't risk missing Michael Stipe.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I knew that Michael Stipe thing was a total lie.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But I still had to Google it just to be sure.

jokes.text: I can't keep referring to basketball players as 'Khloe Kardashian's husband and his friends.'
jokes.setup: Tom needs to learn basketball to connect with customers
jokes.punchline: His hilariously indirect way of referring to NBA players
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific joke that perfectly captures Tom's pop culture obsessions and complete basketball ignorance.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, so I need to learn about basketball. I've been studying the greats. You know, like that tall guy who plays for the Lakers? The one who's really good at dunking? I think his name is... Basketball Dunk Man?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And then there's the guy on the Celtics. Super talented. I call him... Green Shirt Bounce Guy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh! And the Miami Heat player? The one everyone talks about? I'm pretty sure his name is Swish Swish Points Man.

jokes.text: My only plan was to buy Skittles.
jokes.setup: Tom asking them to cancel their plans
jokes.punchline: Chris's extremely simple life plan
jokes.explanation: Shows Chris's simple pleasures and somewhat empty social calendar.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey, can you guys cancel your plans tonight? I need to talk to you about something important.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I can't. I have a very strict schedule. At 6 PM, I'm going to eat a plain chicken breast with steamed vegetables. At 7 PM, I'm going to do some light stretching. At 8 PM, I'm going to review my financial spreadsheets. And at 9 PM, I'm going to be asleep.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's your entire night?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: That's every night. It's called discipline, Tom. It's called living a life of purpose and meaning.

jokes.text: Your gender equality commission is a real sausage fest.
jokes.setup: Commission meeting about gender equality
jokes.punchline: It's entirely men
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony that drives home the episode's theme through contradiction.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: It's entirely men

jokes.text: Oh, there's a lady right next to you. Oh, wait, no. It's just a very beautiful man.
jokes.setup: Leslie pointing out lack of women
jokes.punchline: Milton mistakes an attractive man for a woman
jokes.explanation: Standard misdirection joke that works but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: You know, I've been looking at this council, and I have to say, there's a real lack of women represented here.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? There's a woman right there.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Where?

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: That beautiful blonde woman in the suit.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's Ben Wyatt. He's a man.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: Well, he should be a woman. He's gorgeous.

jokes.text: Round of applause for the girl. She has to leave to get more snacks.
jokes.setup: Discussion about women's roles in government
jokes.punchline: Immediately reduces Leslie to snack-getting role
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor showing exactly the problem Leslie is trying to address.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: Women are perfectly capable in government roles.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: Leslie, be a dear and fetch us some snacks.

jokes.text: It's not the 7th yet.
jokes.setup: Leslie being 'ornery'
jokes.punchline: Milton checking if it's her period using the calendar system
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that shows the calendar system is still in use. Disturbing but very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: Is it that time of the month?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Excuse me?

dialogue.speaker: Councilman Milton
dialogue.line: I'm just checking the calendar. You seem ornery.

jokes.text: Your shoes are red now.
jokes.setup: Ron watching kids play with markers
jokes.punchline: Kid matter-of-factly notes someone's shoes got colored
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag showing consequences of letting kids loose with art supplies.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Why are you children writing on each other?

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: We're not writing on each other. We're coloring his shoes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: His shoes are already a color.

jokes.text: Of course. I'd be delighted.
jokes.setup: Diane asking if Ron can watch kids again
jokes.punchline: Ron's overly formal positive response despite obvious chaos
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron - formal politeness masking what was clearly a difficult experience.

dialogue.speaker: Diane
dialogue.line: Ron, can you watch the kids again this weekend?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Of course, Diane. I would be delighted to spend time with your children. I have prepared a comprehensive schedule of activities involving woodworking, proper etiquette, and early bedtimes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Everything will be perfectly orderly and under control.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Dad, the kitchen's on fire!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That is merely a controlled learning experience about the dangers of negligence.

jokes.text: I believe one problem with hiring women is that they're frail and breakable.
jokes.setup: Discussion about women in the workplace
jokes.punchline: Councilman compares women to fragile objects
jokes.explanation: Shows the absurd reasoning behind workplace discrimination.

dialogue.speaker: Councilman
dialogue.line: Women are like fine china - beautiful to look at, but you can't use them for anything practical.

jokes.text: You have one woman, and she's a secretary. And she's the best secretary we got, except for Dan.
jokes.setup: Defending their hiring practices
jokes.punchline: Reveals their 'best' female employee is still second to a male secretary
jokes.explanation: Good example of how even praise for women gets qualified and diminished.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We have an excellent track record with female employees. Our best female employee is amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Yes, but she's still behind Kevin in the rankings.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Kevin is a male secretary.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: they are the smelly glue that keeps this city together
jokes.setup: Leslie praising garbage collectors
jokes.punchline: Calls them 'smelly glue' - backhanded compliment
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie - earnest praise that accidentally includes an insult.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know, I just want to say that garbage collectors are some of the most important people in our society. They're hardworking, they're dedicated, they're... smelly glue that holds our community together.

jokes.text: You ever found a dead body in the trash? Or body parts? Like, a torso or a head? Please say head.
jokes.setup: April expressing interest in garbage work
jokes.punchline: Her enthusiasm is specifically about finding corpses
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - dark enthusiasm for morbid discoveries. 'Please say head' is particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I want to work in garbage. Not because I care about the environment or whatever, but because I want to find corpses.

jokes.text: 'Pick up garbage. Put in truck'?
jokes.setup: Leslie bringing detailed binders
jokes.punchline: Dewey correctly guessing the overly simple content
jokes.explanation: Good joke about Leslie's tendency to over-organize simple tasks.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I've prepared detailed binders on every topic we could possibly discuss.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Leslie, what's in these binders?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Everything. Comprehensive breakdowns, color-coded sections, backup materials...

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm just going to guess they contain pictures of parks.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: How did you—

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: I know you, Leslie.

jokes.text: You seem more like a Hanson.
jokes.setup: Looking for Chris in directory
jokes.punchline: Casual comment about his appearance/vibe
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about Chris not looking like his actual name.

jokes.text: they're playing with my land mine.
jokes.setup: Chris running out of kid activities
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions having a land mine as a toy option
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Chris moment - casually mentioning having military ordnance as office decoration.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know, we've done soccer, we've done ballet, we've done chess club... I'm running out of activities for the kids.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I mean, at this point I'm like, 'What's next, we could just set up a land mine in the backyard?'

jokes.text: you'd think our boobs would get in the way.
jokes.setup: Leslie and April outperforming male garbage workers
jokes.punchline: Leslie sarcastically referencing sexist assumptions
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic comment that highlights absurd workplace assumptions about women.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wow, so we're doing better at this job than you guys. I guess that means we're better at everything. We should run the country. We should run the world.

dialogue.speaker: Male Garbage Worker
dialogue.line: That's not what this means.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You started this. You implied that physical strength determines someone's worth as a person and as a professional. So clearly, since April and I are kicking your ass, we are now your superiors in every conceivable way.

jokes.text: Google Earth... Always taking pics.
jokes.setup: Reporter asking if she's posing
jokes.punchline: Leslie's paranoid response about satellite photography
jokes.explanation: Funny Leslie paranoia about being photographed, even from space.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No! I'm not posing. Why would I be posing? Do you know how many satellites are up there right now? Forty-seven. I counted. And they're all looking down. Always looking down. I can feel it on the back of my neck.

jokes.text: It's not fair. I feel like everyone should be the same size.
jokes.setup: Andy can't guard Tom because of height difference
jokes.punchline: Andy's childlike solution to competitive disadvantage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - innocent, childlike solution to an unfair situation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I got it. I'm just gonna get really good at basketball really fast.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Like, I'm gonna watch some YouTube videos, do some drills, and by tomorrow I'll be unstoppable.

jokes.text: Did I do basketball? Kind of.
jokes.setup: Tom attempting to play basketball
jokes.punchline: His uncertainty about whether his actions qualified as the sport
jokes.explanation: Good Tom character moment showing his disconnect from athletic reality

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm playing basketball.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Tom, you're just standing there holding the ball.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, but am I though?

jokes.text: Did I do basketball?
jokes.setup: Tom attempting to shoot basketball
jokes.punchline: His uncertain question about whether he succeeded
jokes.explanation: Tom's complete basketball ignorance shown through treating it like an abstract concept.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Did that go in?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I think that went in.

jokes.text: But it was actually a blood-hungry witch who was wearing their mom's face like a mask!
jokes.setup: Ann telling kids a story
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's telling them horrifying horror story
jokes.explanation: Shows Ann's complete misunderstanding of age-appropriate content for children.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Once upon a time, there were three little children who went into the woods...

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: And they never came back. Their bones were found scattered across the forest floor, picked clean by vultures.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: The end.

jokes.text: Sorry. I thought kids liked ghost stories.
jokes.setup: Ron's horrified reaction to her story
jokes.punchline: Ann's innocent explanation for traumatizing children
jokes.explanation: Ann's well-meaning but misguided assumption about children's entertainment preferences.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I was just trying to teach them about the circle of life. I brought in a dead bird I found in my yard and showed it to all the kids. I thought it would be educational!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Ann.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? It's natural! They need to understand that death is part of nature.

jokes.text: I knew that wench wasn't naturally blonde.
jokes.setup: April finding hair dye in someone's trash
jokes.punchline: Her vindictive satisfaction at the discovery
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - taking petty satisfaction in others' secrets revealed through trash.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Hair dye. In the trash. Someone's covering something up.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm gonna find out who, and I'm gonna use it against them. Forever.

jokes.text: She has to use prescription-strength deodorant.
jokes.setup: Leslie joining April in trash snooping
jokes.punchline: Leslie's excited discovery of embarrassing personal information
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie getting caught up in April's vindictive enthusiasm for gossip.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, April, look at this! Ben threw away his high school yearbook with a note that says 'Too many memories of being unpopular.'

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's great. Can we go now?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, there's more! His old gym class photos where he's clearly struggling with the rope climb. This is gold, April. Pure gold.

jokes.text: This is the best day of my life.
jokes.setup: April discovering embarrassing information in trash
jokes.punchline: Her genuine enthusiasm for this petty victory
jokes.explanation: Classic April - finding genuine joy in others' minor misfortunes and secrets.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh my god. Oh my god, oh my god, oh my god. Ben threw away his grocery list and it says 'Activia.' Ben Wyatt drinks Activia.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: This is the best day of my life.

jokes.text: And some privacy to violate! Yeah! Whoo! Wait, no.
jokes.setup: April getting excited about their work
jokes.punchline: April accidentally reveals her unethical motivations, Leslie's horror
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing April's true motivations and Leslie's dawning realization

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm so excited about this project. I've been thinking about it all night.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's wonderful! What specifically has you so energized?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Well, I realized if we push this through, we can use the city funds to build that statue of our enemy in the town square so everyone has to look at his stupid face every day.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April... that's not— we can't just use public resources for personal vendettas.

jokes.text: This could really only go poorly.
jokes.setup: Tom about to play basketball with actual kids
jokes.punchline: Ben's resigned prediction
jokes.explanation: Ben's practical pessimism about Tom's athletic abilities against actual players.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: He's going to pull a hamstring and then sue the city.

jokes.text: I told you you can pivot, not that you can only pivot.
jokes.setup: Tom constantly pivoting during basketball
jokes.punchline: Ben's frustrated clarification of basketball rules
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup showing Tom's misunderstanding of basketball fundamentals.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Tom, you can't just pivot whenever you want. That's traveling.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: But I'm pivoting!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yes, I can see that. That's the problem.

jokes.text: You suck at basketball, man.
jokes.setup: Tom's terrible basketball performance
jokes.punchline: Kid's brutal honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Kids' honest assessment provides punchline to Tom's failed athletic display.

dialogue.speaker: Kid
dialogue.line: You're really bad at basketball.

jokes.text: Well, you suck at being polite, sir.
jokes.setup: Kid insulting Tom's basketball skills
jokes.punchline: Tom's formal, dignified response to child's insult
jokes.explanation: Tom's characteristic formal politeness even when insulted by children.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: You're bad at basketball!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate your passion for the sport. However, I would submit that my performance today was hampered by several external factors, including but not limited to: suboptimal court conditions, inadequate footwear, and the general chaos of this recreational facility. I'll have you know I was MVP of my church league in 2009.

jokes.text: Stand back, ladies! Ron, no, no, no, no! That's dangerous!
jokes.setup: Ron preparing to break down door
jokes.punchline: Ann's horror at his extreme solution to simple problem
jokes.explanation: Good Ron character moment showing his tendency toward extreme solutions

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm going to break down this door.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? No! Ron, we can just call the landlord!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I am the landlord now.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're not the landlord!

jokes.text: Her hair is infected.
jokes.setup: Kids playing doctor with dolls
jokes.punchline: Kid's medical diagnosis for why doll needs haircut
jokes.explanation: Kids' creative medical reasoning for destructive behavior with scissors.

dialogue.speaker: Kid
dialogue.line: The doll has a severe case of bad hair day syndrome. It's terminal. The only cure is a haircut.

jokes.text: That's the covergirl slogan. I didn't mean to say that.
jokes.setup: Leslie saying 'easy, breezy, beautiful' while lifting fridge
jokes.punchline: Her immediate recognition and embarrassment about quoting makeup ad
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment showing her pop culture knowledge and immediate self-consciousness.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Easy, breezy, beautiful... CoverGirl.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, why did I just say that?

jokes.text: What, you think Joan of Arc needed a hand?
jokes.setup: Being offered help with the fridge
jokes.punchline: Leslie's historical comparison for refusing assistance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - turning garbage collection into historical feminist statement.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, I got this. I'm like Rosa Parks, but for fridges.

jokes.text: I don't know why I thought sneaking up on it would work.
jokes.setup: Leslie struggling with the heavy fridge
jokes.punchline: Her attempt at stealth approach to moving heavy object
jokes.explanation: Funny desperation move showing Leslie's increasingly irrational problem-solving.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, if I just... tip it back on the dolly wheels... and kind of shimmy it sideways...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: See? Stealth. Like a ninja. A very loud, grunting ninja.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Why is this so heavy? Did they fill it with sadness?

jokes.text: Did you just say you love Diane?
jokes.setup: Ron accidentally revealing his feelings
jokes.punchline: Ann catching his slip
jokes.explanation: Standard romantic comedy moment of accidental revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Ann, you are the most competent, dedicated, and... and beautiful woman I've ever met.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Ron, did you just call me beautiful?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No. I said you were a... beautiful disaster. Of paperwork.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's not what you said.

jokes.text: For God's sake, Hanson, will you please focus on the larger problem?
jokes.setup: Ann getting excited about Ron's love confession
jokes.punchline: Ron using wrong name while trying to deflect from romantic topic
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Ron's flustered state after romantic slip-up.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Ron, I heard what you said. You love me!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I said no such thing, Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You just called me Leslie!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Did I? Well, that changes nothing.

jokes.text: Ron loves Mommy!
jokes.setup: Kids overhearing Ron's confession
jokes.punchline: They immediately start chanting about it
jokes.explanation: Kids' innocent but embarrassing announcement of adult romance.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I have a deeply rooted fear of commitment.

dialogue.speaker: Kids
dialogue.line: Ron's scared! Ron's scared! Ron's scared of commitment!

jokes.text: Ron loves Mommy! Ron loves Mommy! I love nothing!
jokes.setup: Kids overhearing Ron's confession
jokes.punchline: Ron's increasingly desperate denials
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with perfect Ron ending - 'I love nothing!' is quintessential character voice

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I love breakfast food.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That doesn't mean I don't love meat.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I love 'em both equally.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Don't tell anyone.

jokes.text: I love nothing!
jokes.setup: Kids chanting about Ron loving their mom
jokes.punchline: Ron's desperate denial that contradicts his character growth
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron panic response - overcorrecting to his old emotionless persona.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't love her!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I mean, I respect her work ethic, her intelligence, her unwavering dedication to public service...

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: But that's not love. That's just... admiration. Professional admiration.

jokes.text: my sister, a few people from work, and my ex-boyfriend.
jokes.setup: Shauna suggesting a romantic dinner
jokes.punchline: Reveals it would include a crowd of people including her ex
jokes.explanation: Shows the continued confusion about their relationship status through terrible date planning.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: I'm thinking we could have a really romantic dinner, just the two of us.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: Well, my ex will be there, and probably like fifteen of my closest friends, and maybe some people from my CrossFit gym...

jokes.text: Maybe we should just eat the refrigerator.
jokes.setup: Leslie's desperation about moving the fridge
jokes.punchline: Suggests consuming it as a solution
jokes.explanation: Leslie's characteristic determination taken to absurd extremes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We can't move the fridge. It's too heavy. So I guess we'll just have to eat all the food in it.

jokes.text: Are you a ghost? Yes, we are. Why you think you can lift it? You are two little canaries.
jokes.setup: Strange man appears to explain the refrigerator situation
jokes.punchline: April deadpan confirms they're ghosts, man compares them to small birds
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment with April's deadpan ghost confirmation and animal size metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Orin
dialogue.line: You see, what happened is you're all ghosts. You died in this house and now you're trapped here forever.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, what? That's not... we're not ghosts.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're ghosts.

dialogue.speaker: Orin
dialogue.line: Yes, you're like small birds trapped in a house. Except you're dead.

jokes.text: So if bears can't lift the-- No, I understand animal sizes.
jokes.setup: Owner explaining bear/canary size comparison
jokes.punchline: Leslie's frustrated interruption
jokes.explanation: Leslie's impatience with condescending animal metaphors is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bakery owner
dialogue.line: A bear is much bigger than a canary.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes, we all know that! Can we please just get back to the cupcakes?

jokes.text: Look, all we have to do is get some PCP. You can move anything on angel dust.
jokes.setup: April suggesting solution to fridge problem
jokes.punchline: Proposes illegal drugs as answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect April solution - practical but completely inappropriate and illegal.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Have you tried putting illegal drugs in it?

jokes.text: Can I borrow $1,500? You're not allowed to ask what it's for. Fireworks.
jokes.setup: Tom asking for money with no questions
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals it's for fireworks anyway
jokes.explanation: Good Tom character moment showing his inability to keep secrets or be mysterious

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey Ben, can I borrow some money? No questions asked.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Uh, sure. What's it for?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Fireworks.

jokes.text: Once, Zoe put Ivy in the dryer, so...
jokes.setup: Diane responding to Ron's apology about children's behavior
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions previous dangerous incident to provide perspective
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan parenting humor showing Diane's experienced perspective on child chaos

dialogue.speaker: Diane
dialogue.line: Well, Ron, last week one of them set the kitchen on fire trying to make toast. So compared to that, this is fine.

jokes.text: And it's trash. It's filled with trash.
jokes.setup: Leslie opening April's 'gift'
jokes.punchline: Discovers it's literally garbage
jokes.explanation: April's characteristic mean-spirited 'gift' that's thematically appropriate to the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, April, a gift! How thoughtful!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Literally garbage. I found it in the dumpster behind the Sweetums building.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Now, that's basketball.
jokes.setup: Tom's commercial for his store
jokes.punchline: Concludes with basketball reference despite knowing nothing about basketball
jokes.explanation: Tom's confident conclusion despite having learned absolutely nothing about basketball.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Welcome to Tom's Bistro. We have the finest cuisine in Pawnee. Our chef trained in Paris for twelve years. We also have a full bar with craft cocktails made by mixologists who actually know what they're doing. And if you're not satisfied with your meal, we will personally fly to your house and cook you another one. Tom's Bistro: nothing but net.

tmdb_overview: Leslie and April handle a garbage route, while Tom asks Andy and Ben to teach him about basketball. Meanwhile, Ron is left to watch Diane's kids while their babysitter is out of town.
insight: Women in garbage lands 84 through character-driven comedy, 70 jokes in 25 minutes.
